data.title: Garden Of EDEN: Despite Turbulent Greenland Geopolitics, Denmark Is A Buy
data.description:
Thesis

The iShares MSCI Denmark ETF (EDEN) offers attractive overall fundamentals, and Denmark’s economy remains one of the strongest economies in Europe. Low sovereign debt, a relatively young population (by European standards), reliance on a national currency, and other factors leave me feeling bullish about Denmark’s near- and medium-term prospects.

Given the fundamentals and macro tailwinds, I assign EDEN a buy rating. The issue with Greenland certainly is a threat and could upend markets globally. Yet in terms of the direct impact, Greenland is currently a drain on Denmark financially speaking, which would mitigate some of the damage should Copenhagen lose the island. I don’t expect the Trump Administration to take Greenland by force, but either way, belligerence will likely lead to Europe striving to depend less on America.

Rally Round Denmark

President Trump’s surprising aggression towards Denmark and the EU as a whole is likely to fuel a circle-the-wagons response. The United States and the EU (plus the UK) had reached a general understanding and framework for a trade deal just months ago. Yet now a new round of tariff wars may be upon us. Trade isn’t the focus this time around, true, but the fallout from the Greenland spat could quickly sink markets and upend relations between close allies.

I believe we’re going to see a sustained cooling of relations between Europe and the United States. Of course, if the United States forcefully takes Greenland, relations are probably toast, and long-term decoupling would likely unfold. Even if the Trump Administration steps back, however, I expect to see a substantial push in Europe to increase self-sufficiency and to rebalance power to even the playing field.

This is more of a hunch or anecdote, but it seems to me that when certain countries get singled out amid geopolitical drama, there’s a tendency for allies to go out of their way to help them. With Denmark, in particular, under threat from the Trump Administration, I believe European allies will go out of their way to provide support. Both at the EU level and through bilateral relationships, I suspect Denmark will enjoy a fair bit of sympathy. This might result in favorable investments, trade, and more.

Still, while Denmark is the focus of this article, the covered ETF, and the current geopolitical battle, the likely changes stemming from the Greenland spat will cross many borders. Among other things, the Trump Administration is proving unpredictable and disruptive. That’s bad not only for governments but also for businesses and investors. In response to repeated escalations with the Trump Administration, I believe we’ll see the EU as a whole drifting further away from the United States, with improved self-sufficiency now being a primary and perhaps existential aim.

Certainly, the mood among the European public is souring. Only 16% of Europeans viewed the United States as an ally in a November poll, before the Greenland drama flared up. 20% view America as a rival or enemy. A December poll by Politico found similar results. 52% of German and 47% of French adults believe the United States creates problems, while only 22% of Germans and 29% of French adults believe they solve problems.

How Would The Loss Of Greenland Affect Markets, Denmark, and EDEN?

I don’t believe President Trump is serious about seizing Greenland. That said, the president has proven me wrong before and has surprised me, too. As such, I certainly wouldn’t rule out Greenland being seized by force. If this does occur, markets globally will almost certainly plummet. Safe haven assets, like gold, would likely surge as investors look for off-ramps. I expect European stock markets to be hit the hardest initially, but the United States would sink rapidly as well. I’d expect non-EU and non-USA assets to recover more quickly, and China in particular could look all the more attractive.

Denmark’s stock markets would almost certainly be among the hardest hit, initially, which would weigh EDEN down. Yet interestingly, the loss of Greenland itself wouldn’t have much of an impact on Denmark from the perspective of GDP, resources, etc. Greenland is actually a drain on Denmark, with Copenhagen sending around $1 billion in aid to Greenland while getting little (financially speaking) in return. Theoretically, Greenland is flush with natural resources, but the harsh weather, glaciers, and other factors result in that natural wealth being locked away. It may be decades or more until extracting natural resources in Greenland en masse is economically viable.

Greenland currently exports a fair bit of seafood, but the total value is less than $2 billion. At least in the here and now, losing Greenland strictly from the perspective of a spreadsheet might actually be an economic benefit. Of course, this doesn’t consider Greenland’s vast but hard-to-reach natural resources. Further, the likely market meltdown wouldn’t be due to losing Greenland, per se, but instead the resulting geopolitical fallout that could upend world order.

EDEN Fundamentals And Valuation Considerations

In order to provide context, I’ll compare EDEN to iShares MSCI Germany ETF (EWG), as Germany is often considered the strongest European economy. Comparing EDEN to the S&P 500 (SPY) provides further context. With that in mind, EDEN’s portfolio enjoys a 16.66 PE ratio (Morningstar), to EWG’s 15.28 and SPY’s 22.35. The Price/Sales ratio for EDEN weighs in at 1.44, which is quite reasonable compared to SPY’s 3.17 but a bit above EWG’s 1.22. These PE and PS ratios are higher than what you typically find in Southern Europe, but Denmark’s economy overall is more heavily focused on growth and technology.

EDEN’s portfolio is a bit more highly concentrated than I prefer, with the top 10 holdings making up more than 65% of the portfolio weight. There are 49 holdings, according to Seeking Alpha data. Given the high concentration in Novo Nordisk, which makes up +23% of the portfolio, I believe it’s important to take a quick look at the company individually.

Novo Nordisk Quick Fundamentals

Novo Nordisk (NYSE:NVO) is a major pharmaceutical company with its GLP-1 medications getting a lot of attention. These medications are proving highly effective at helping people lose weight. On an individual level, many people have a strong drive to get in shape. Governments too have generally been supportive of GLP-1 medications, as obese and overweight citizens typically require more healthcare spending. NVO is enjoying strong growth, with revenues up 16.64% (YOY). The net income margin is quite high at 32.88%, and the GAAP PE (TTM) ratio is reasonable at 16.04, compared to a sector median of 27.35.

I am not ready to offer a full individual rating for NVO, but the basic fundamentals look strong, and GLP-1 has the potential to turn into a reliable, profitable cash cow. The +20% portfolio weight does make me a bit nervous.

Stepping back to consider the whole portfolio, EDEN offers solid dividends at 2.60%. If my theory that Europe is going to shift inward is correct, I think we’ll see some European companies restrain dividends to free up resources to invest. I focus more on value than income, but I’d be careful about relying on EDEN as an income investment.

Costs are reasonable, by the way, with the adjusted cost ratio at .53%, but that is a bit above EWG’s .49%. Still, these international ETFs often have adjusted expense ratios exceeding .6%. Tracking errors are also quite high with international ETFs, with rates often topping 20%. EDEN is in decent shape with a 13.76% 1-year tracking error and an 11.81% 5-year tracking error. These numbers don’t wow me, but I’m not uncomfortable with them either.

All in all, the fundamentals here seem strong. The portfolio is reasonably diverse in terms of sectors, which I think is especially important for broad macro-focused theses, such as my own. Since I’m bullish regarding Denmark’s overall economy, and given reasonable valuation metrics, I assign EDEN a buy rating. We’ll probably see some dips in the weeks ahead, so long as Greenland remains a source of discontent, but that could create attractive entry points.

Why I Could Be Wrong

First, President Trump may make good on his threats to take Greenland by force, which would likely sink markets across the globe. Denmark and EDEN would take an especially big hit, I suspect. Even after markets stabilize, the fallout in this scenario would likely be a drag on the world economy for many years to come.

Second, my bullish sentiments are predicated on the idea that Europe will emphasize self-sufficiency going forward. Europe faces myriad challenges, however, and long-term repositioning could create short-term pain. This could affect markets, but more concerningly, if European leaders take their foot off the gas, long-term risks may continue to mount and grow more grave.

Key Takeaway: Denmark Is A Buy, Making Eden A Buy

Uncertainty is the only thing certain in these uncertain times. Denmark, Greenland, the EU, and wider Europe will have to confront a shake-up in global order. However, I believe these challenges could ultimately make Europe stronger by encouraging internal focus, renewed effort, and an existential need to improve self-sufficiency. Against this backdrop, Denmark’s prospects look especially good. EDEN, in turn, is attractive, with solid fundamentals and reasonable valuations.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: I'm Bullish On Diana Shipping After The Genco Proxy Fight (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

Diana Shipping Inc. (DSX) is a Greece-based dry bulk carrier hauling iron ore, coal, and grain around the world. The last time I wrote about it, I had the stock at a Hold: it looked cheap, but not cheap enough to justify a Buy while weak freight rates were steadily eating away at earnings power.

In retrospect, DSX’s momentum’s been on the right track, now almost par with the broader market (SP500). In this update, after reviewing Diana’s move to replace the Genco Shipping & Trading Limited (GNK) board in an escalating takeover fight, I’m leaning bullish on the shares as activism targets NAV upside through consolidation.

Diana Shipping: Forcing Strategic Change

With roughly 14.8% of Genco’s shares in hand, Diana Shipping's signaling that it has real skin in the outcome. After Genco’s board flatly rejected its unsolicited all-cash offer of $20.60 per share, the company escalated its campaign. Last week, it nominated six independent directors, putting forward a full slate for the board. As best as I can tell, it’s an activist play, carefully planned and aimed at seating a board willing to pursue the value-boosting deal logic Diana Shipping considers obvious.

The dry bulk market is still very fragmented, meaning there are hundreds of ship operators all competing for the same types of cargo, such as iron ore, coal, grains, and minor bulks. All things being equal, larger fleets have more bargaining power with charterers, can secure better long-term contracts, and, most importantly, can spread fixed costs.

Diana operates about 36 vessels, and they’re relatively young, with an average age under 12 years, while total capacity is roughly 4.1 million deadweight tons. And the assets are actually working. Utilization basically maxed out at 99.5% last quarter and by late last year, they’d already locked in about $149 million of contracted revenue. Moreover, the charter book is deliberately staggered, averaging $16,200 per day with durations of more than one year. Recent results and fixing activity reinforces the point.

As of November 12, 2025, (Diana has) secured USD 25.4 million of contracted revenues for 87% of the remaining ownership days of the year 2025 and have secured USD 118 million of contracted revenues for 50% of the ownership days of the year 2026. Finally, (Diana is) pleased to declare a quarterly cash dividend of $0.01 per common share with respect to the third quarter of 2025, totaling approximately USD 1.16 million. (please see dividend history stats below)."

Between July and November 2025, Diana Shipping fixed 14 vessels, with Ultramaxes securing average rates of $13,800 per day for roughly 333 days with Panamax, Kamsarmax and Post-Panamax vessels at about $12,900 per day for 331 days. And Capesize and Newcastlemax vessels at approximately $24,500 per day for 380 days.

All things considered, like I stated in the beginning, Genco rejected the proposal, saying it undervalued the company and involved execution risks. In response, Genco suggested a broad and undefined alternative in which Genco would acquire Diana through a reverse takeover using a mix of cash and stock. But it didn't provide any details about pricing, structure, or a premium. Now Diana turned down their idea, saying it was a distraction, and noted that Genco took more than six weeks to reply and never engaged in any discussions about valuation or deal structure.

So with negotiations effectively at an impasse, Diana’s focus turned to governance. Just look at its nominees, people like Gustave Brun-Lie, Jens Ismar, Quentin Soanes, industry veterans with long track records in dry bulk, finance, and law. It signals a board refresh with intent. And more importantly, it puts pressure on the company to seriously evaluate consolidation options. In the meantime, Genco’s board says it will review the nominees through its usual process and lay out its recommendation in the proxy statement ahead of the 2026 annual meeting, which has yet to be scheduled.

Plenty Of Value, Plenty To Go Wrong

At $1.98, DSX is trading at about 3.6x operating cash flow. That’s cheap. Especially when the broader growth backdrop is closer to 15x, and even its own normal multiple is around 7.6x.

Meanwhile, at about 0.44x book value, DSX is trading far below the market value of its ships, even after accounting for the fact that its debt level is meaningful, with long-term debt at nearly 57% of total capital. That discount exists despite the stock throwing off a near 20% operating cash flow yield.

That said, risks are all over the place. For one thing, if Chinese import demand keeps cooling, rates could get hit. At the same time, fleet supply is set to grow about 3.4% in 2026, which doesn’t help. Put those together and day rates could slide. If vessels then refix below the roughly $16,800 breakeven, you’re probably looking at cash burn.

Speaking of which, the cash flow statement is where the risk actually shows up.

Right now, DSX is still throwing off about $60 million of operating cash flow. Free cash flow is positive too, even though a big chunk of the fleet is fixed around the mid-$16,000s per day. That helps. And it comes from the aforementioned high utilization, staggered charters, and very light capex, which is partly funded by recent vessel sales. Still, that cushion isn’t permanent. If rates stay below breakeven for any length of time as contracts roll off, free cash flow flips negative pretty fast.

That risk is higher because capital is tied up in the Genco stake, and I don't see much room left to stretch the balance sheet further. I think with the current discounted price, the activist strategy needs to accelerate value realization. But if the dispute drags on, the company continues to suffer, and the path back toward NAV becomes slow and uncertain.

Final Takeaway

I’m upgrading DSX to a highly speculative Buy. I think there’s upside if the activists force consolidation and the stock rerates closer to NAV. At the same time, the downside looks cushioned, since a chunk of the cash flow is contracted and the assets have real value. So while it’s not risk-free, the risk/reward looks skewed the right way.

data.title: Occidental Petroleum: Higher Gas Prices, Dividend Hike & Divestiture Tailwinds Ahead
data.description:
I previously covered Occidental Petroleum Corporation (OXY) in September 2025, discussing how they had demonstrated their ability to drive improved capital efficiency, as observed in the lowered FY2025 capex and the raised FQ3'25 production guidance.

Combined with the reduction of per-barrel cost/the richer cash flows in H1'25, the accelerated deleveraging cadence, and the divestiture of non-core, largely non-operated assets in its US onshore business, allowing it to largely streamline its operations, I had reiterated my Buy rating near to its established floor at the $36s then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the OXY stock near the December 2025 floor of $38, attributed to the potential FQ4'25 outperformance arising from the higher gas spot prices (well balancing the lower oil prices) and the likely dividend hike sometime in early 2026.

This is on top of the accelerated deleveraging progress likely triggering the adj EPS outperformance and the healthier balance sheet from FQ1'26 onwards, thanks to the recently completed OxyChem divestiture.

OXY's Prospects Discussed Entering FQ4'25 & FY2026

Since my last Buy rating, OXY has already traded along the volatile Brent oil spot prices of between $60s and $65s since Q4'25, with the stock previously nearing my Buy Zones of $36s during the worst of the December 2025 selloff to the $38s.

This is an unsurprising development indeed, given its upstream-focused oil/gas operations at 85.6% of its YTD incomes (+13.6 points YoY), with the balance derived from the midstream operations.

Moving forward. OXY was set to report their FQ4'25 earnings performance on February 19, 2026; I will be discussing numerous important performance metrics for readers to focus on.

1. Realized Prices

Given that its upstream segment is highly sensitive to the fluctuating spot prices, readers may want to monitor OXY's:

  • realized oil prices in FQ4'25 against the $64.78 per barrel reported in FQ3'25 (+1.5% QoQ/-14% YoY),
  • realized NGL prices against the $19.60 per barrel in FQ3'25 (-5.3% QoQ/-4.2% YoY), and
  • realized natural gas prices against the $1.57 per MCF in FQ3'25 (+7.5% QoQ/+106.5% YoY).

These metrics are important indeed, since OXY's overall production capacity is split rather equally between oil at ~50.9% and NGL/natural gas at ~49% in FQ3'25, with the latter recently enjoying higher spot prices at an average price of approximately $3.74 per Mbtu in Q4'25 (+23.8% QoQ/+53.2% YoY).

This is thanks to the increased deployment of gas-powered power plants at a time of data center boom - a trend that has benefitted many players, including Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) and Energy Transfer LP (ET), as numerous hyperscalers and data center REITs pursue renewable energies and even nuclear reactors as their source of reliable/clean energy as part of their ESG strategies.

This tailwind may very well balance the headwinds observed in the oil segment, attributed to the oversupply risks arising from the OPEC+ raised output in 2025 and the growing US crude oil output by +2.5% YoY as of October 2025, since these two players comprise over half of the global oil producers by capacity (US at 22% and OPEC+ at 35%).

With oversupply presenting a real risk entering 2026, it is unsurprising that the US Energy Information Administration [EIA] already expects "the Brent spot price will average $56/b in 2026 and $54/b in 2027, compared with an average of $69/b in 2025."

2. Capital Efficiency

Otherwise, readers must note that OXY is one of the more capital-efficient E&P players, as observed in the majority of its U.S. Onshore Unconventional Gross Operated Inventory, boasting a low breakeven price range at below <$50 per barrel.

This is compared to the "average breakeven price was $62/b in the Permian Midland Basin and $64/b in the Permian Delaware Basin, the two largest basins in the Permian, according to data from a Dallas Fed Energy survey."

This implies OXY's ability to remain profitable on a Free Cash Flow basis despite the potential oil oversupply risks entering 2026.

The same has been similarly observed in the lower oil & gas revenues of $5.4B in FQ3'25 (-5% YoY), with the higher overall production of 1,227 MBOE/D (+3.4% YoY) and the higher natural gas prices unable to balance the lower realized oil prices as discussed above.

This is, albeit, with FQ3'25 bringing forth a more than decent Free Cash Flow generation at $1.47B (-2.6% YoY) despite the higher capex at $1.72B (+5.5% YoY) and the notable top-line erosion, with it lending credence to my Free Cash Flow conjecture above.

The same has been observed in OXY's FY2026 production growth guidance of up to +2% YoY alongside the FY2026 capital spending guidance of $6.5B at the midpoint (+3.1% YoY, after omitting the OxyChem $900M capex), with these numbers lending credence to their efficient capital allocation efforts thus far.

3. Balance Sheet Health

At the same time, readers must note that OxyChem's $9.7B divestiture recently completed in early January 2026 has contributed to the accelerated deleveraging progress below OXY's target of <$15B at an Expected Post-Transaction Debt Balance of $14.3B (-42.5% YoY from FQ3'24 net debt of $24.87B).

This development is likely to trigger an "opportunistic multi-year share repurchase program" beyond current levels of 1,003.1M (-0.7% QoQ), potentially nearer to FQ4'24 lows of 940.8M.

This is on top of OXY's potentially raised dividend payouts beyond the current levels of $0.96 per share, based on the last two hikes by +9.1% in February 2025 and +22.2% in December 2023.

This may potentially trigger an expansion in its forward dividend yield beyond the current levels of 2.25%, compared to the sector median of 3.82% and its direct oil/gas peers, including ConocoPhillips (COP) at 3.42%, XOM at 3.17%, Chevron (CVX) at 4.11%, and Devon (DVN) at 2.65%.

As a result of the accelerated deleveraging progress along with the realization of ">$350 MM of annual interest expense savings," I posit that FQ1'26 is likely to bring forth a sequentially healthier balance sheet along with an excellent adj EPS performance, with it building upon OXY's potential FQ4'25 outperformance arising from the higher gas spot prices, as discussed in the first section.

OXY Remains a Great Buy Upon a Dip

For now, OXY has been seemingly well defended at the Q4'25 trading floor of $39s, with the stock currently recovering along the higher Brent/gas prices albeit seemingly unable to break out of the $44s resistance levels in January 2026.

Despite the stock price volatility, I am of the opinion that OXY remains fairly valued at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 18.84x and FWD Price/Cash Flow valuations of 3.90x, compared to its pre-pandemic mean of 20.93x/10.07x and its oil/gas peers, including:

  • upstream - COP at 15.59x/6.17x,
  • diversified - XOM at 18.69x/9.85x, and
  • diversified - CVX at 22.87x/9.02x,
  • aside from upstream - DVN at 9.05x/3.50x, respectively.

When compared to its peers, it is apparent that OXY remains cheaply valued at my estimated Enterprise Value to Proven Reserve ratio of 15.52x as well.

This is based on OXY's Enterprise Value of $71.58B by the time of writing and the 2024 proved reserves of 4.61M BOE (+15.8% YoY), against COP at 17.74x, XOM at 29.27x, CVX at 38.19x, and DVN at 14x, using the same methodology.

As a result of the potential FQ4'25 outperformance arising from the higher gas spot prices, the likely dividend hike in early 2026, along with the accelerated deleveraging progress likely triggering the adj EPS outperformance and the healthier balance sheet from FQ1'26 onwards, I am cautiously reiterating my Buy rating for the OXY stock, albeit with the caveat that investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer.

My caution is attributed to the seemingly reversing momentum after the recent inability to break out of the $44 resistance levels, with the stock potentially returning some of its recent gains to bounce off the $39 support levels.

Given the excellent dip-buying opportunity upon the -8.6% correction from current levels and the consequently richer dividend yields at approximately ~2.4%, some patience may be quite prudent in the meantime.

data.title: Intel Has A New Problem
data.description:
As discussed in my previous coverage on Intel Corporation (INTC), its lack of execution consistency remains the biggest Achilles’ heel to its turnaround strategy over the past several years. Despite the stock’s more than 81% upsurge since Lip-Bu Tan assumed the role of CEO last year, much of the upside has been driven by external validation rather than tangible operational progress. This includes an unprecedented $8.9 billion investment made by the U.S. government in August, which translated into an approximately 10% stake in the company; a subsequent $2 billion investment by SoftBank Group Corp. (SFTBY)(SFTBF)(SOBKY); and a $5 billion investment by AI darling NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) in September, complemented by a strategic collaboration on next-generation PC and data center processors. Yet the high-profile investments from industry partners and favourable policy support from the U.S. government have yet to translate into tangible follow-up results in Intel’s core operations.

The disconnect accordingly makes 2026 an urgent year for delivery. This is corroborated by the stock’s more than 150% recovery since its August 2025 low, reflecting heightened investors’ expectations around Foundry’s execution, in particular. Specifically, Intel Foundry has been a critical component in Intel’s years-long comeback plan since the Pat Gelsinger era, and recently maintained under Tan’s leadership. Despite having executed on the “five nodes in four years” (“5N4Y”) strategy, the returns on Intel Foundry’s inherently capital-intensive buildout remain uncertain. The unit’s trajectory to breakeven on its latest 18A node is still heavily reliant on internal Intel Products volumes, while progress on securing external customer uptake remains scarce, limiting its earnings durability.

And true to Intel’s experience, new complications are emerging at the worst timing possible. In the latest development, tightening global memory supply could represent an incremental headwind to Intel’s turnaround plans. Specifically, heightened demand for DRAM-based high-bandwidth memory (“HBM”) used in advanced AI accelerators has effectively restricted manufacturing capacity for other memory types. And the subsequent industry supply crunch now underscores a protracted trajectory of “memory cost inflation” ahead. This dynamic could introduce material execution risks to the Client Computing Group’s (“CCG”) roadmap, which currently represents Intel’s primary growth driver and FCF engine needed to fund both its R&D and Foundry’s buildout.

Looking ahead, Intel may be facing a familiar problem of added execution pressure that stems from both internal mismanagement and external industry shocks beyond its control. This risks a repeat of past mishaps, including the delayed go-to-market of Sapphire Rapids that shipped into a cyclical downturn and resulted in permanent server CPU share loss to rival Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) during the cloud buildout; and a Gaudi accelerator roadmap that lacked resonation with customer needs at the height of the AI compute supercycle, leaving Intel structurally disadvantaged in the rapidly scaling data center GPU market.

Taken together, these parallels heighten the stakes for Intel as its Q4 2025 earnings update approaches later this week (January 22). This is expected to draw intensified investors’ scrutiny on its execution progress and reinforce urgency for the company to deliver tangible results on its turnaround strategy in the year ahead.

Intel’s Memory Problem

The client compute industry, which includes the PC market critical to Intel, faces an increasingly disruptive operating backdrop ahead of the tightening global memory supply crunch. As I’ve extensively discussed in recent coverage on Sandisk Corporation (SNDK) and Micron Technology, Inc. (MU), the accelerating AI infrastructure buildout is creating an increasing imbalance in global memory production capacity.

Specifically, DRAM-based HBM, a critical component in AI processors, now commands the dominant share of existing memory output. This has essentially diminished production capacity for other memory types, such as NAND flash, critical in client compute applications. The supply dynamic is corroborated by Micron management’s recent remarks, citing that dominant HBM demand has essentially triggered a worsening “shortage of [memory for] the conventional side of the industry, for phones or PCs”. And emerging use cases, spanning autonomous vehicles and humanoid robots, are poised to further intensify memory demand in the longer term.

The immediate consequence is evident in surging prices across both NAND and DRAM memory observed in recent months. Specifically, the industry average selling price (“ASP”) surge for DRAM has been most prominent, increasing by 40% sequentially during the December quarter, as accelerating HBM-driven demand continues to outpace supply. This is accompanied by a 20% sequential NAND ASP expansion over the same period, as limited supply availability – squeezed by DRAM capacity prioritization – coincides with increasing bit demand growth to address rising memory content requirements in AI-enhanced client compute devices. The emerging risk of protracted memory cost inflation is corroborated by recent warnings by key PC OEMs like Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL) and HP Inc. (HPQ):

Looking ahead, our guidance reflects the increasing inflationary pressures predicted for memory costs, which we expect at this point to have an impact as we move into the back half of our fiscal year…Therefore, we are taking a prudent approach to our guide while implementing aggressive actions to mitigate this. They include qualifying lower-cost suppliers and redesigning the portfolio for reduced memory configurations, accelerating our AI-enabled transformation to drive further cost savings, and raising prices in close partnerships with our channel and direct customers.

Source: HP Inc. Q4 Fiscal 2025 Earnings Call Transcript

Look, we're in a very unique time. It's unprecedented. We have not seen costs move at the rate that we've seen. And by the way, it's not unique to DRAM. It's NAND. It is hard drives, leading-edge nodes across the semiconductor network. There is a -- if you will, I'd categorize it as demand is way ahead of supply…Our direct model allows us to act on the market signals it gives us quicker than anybody else, allows us to price accordingly, and reprice when needed. And we will make our way through that across consumer PCs, commercial PCs, into server storage, and through our AI servers. No product category is going to be impacted in terms of the aggregate cost basis moving.

Source: Dell Q3 Fiscal 2026 Earnings Call Transcript

With memory currently accounting for “15% to 18% of the cost of a typical PC”, the industry expects key OEMs to implement price actions of 15% to 20% on average in the year ahead. Meanwhile, Dell is mulling price hikes of as much as 30% on its “laptop and desktop models with higher amounts of RAM or SSD storage capacity” to offset the impending memory cost headwind.

The emerging memory cost inflation and ensuing pass-through to consumers are expected to derail the industry’s PC recovery narrative, just as edge AI begins to scale alongside Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) Windows 10’s “end-of-life” refresh cycle. Specifically, industry forecasts now project declines of 2.4% to 8.9% y/y in total PC shipments this year, with impending price hikes to be offset by volatility in memory supply and cost headwinds. This is corroborated by HP’s recent warning of PC unit TAM compression this year, potentially offsetting revenue TAM growth expected in the low-single digit percentage range.

More importantly, this dynamic risks becoming structural, as the reallocation of memory production supply increasingly prioritizes higher-margin, AI-centric solutions like DRAM-based HBM for data center applications. These protracted memory supply limitations effectively introduce a material risk to Intel’s client-centric execution path in the near term, which could also spill over to its Foundry ROI prospects.

2 Downside Risk Implications for Intel

1. CCG Growth Constraints and Earnings Deterioration

Intel’s CCG remains the company’s key growth and earnings driver. This has inadvertently made the unit a critical source of FCF to fund Intel’s ongoing R&D roadmap, as well as its capital-intensive Foundry strategy maintained under Tan’s leadership.

Specifically, the unit accounted for more than 62.5% of Intel’s total revenue mix in Q3. It’s also the most profitable and only margin-accretive unit across Intel’s consolidated operations. CCG drove 88.5% of Intel Products’ operating income in Q3 and delivered an operating margin of close to 36.0%. While Intel doesn’t separately bifurcate its FCF by segment, it’s evident based on its earnings profile that CCG represents the core source.

This accordingly heightens Intel’s exposure to impending disruptions in the PC end-market, reinforcing the adverse implications of Intel’s inherent revenue concentration risk discussed in my previous coverage. The looming PC declines this year, driven by intensifying memory supply constraints and ensuing inflationary headwinds, risk direct impairment to CCG’s growth trajectory and earnings profile. It’d also risk undermining management’s earlier optimism on the forward PC demand outlook. This was reflected in their latest guidance for “client consumption TAM to approach 290 million units in 2025” and preparations for “another year of strong demand in 2026”, given expectations for Panther Lake to ramp into a “healthy PC ecosystem”. The emerging memory headwind essentially comes at an inopportune time, given Intel’s heightened reliance on CCG to fund its R&D, Foundry roadmap, and broader deleveraging efforts.

The impending headwind is further compounded by increasing risks of misalignment in CCG’s near-term growth strategy and current market dynamics. Specifically, Intel has made CCG’s product portfolio a strategic priority in 2026. Yet much of the upcoming refresh now risks shipping into a downturn due to unfavourable memory supply conditions and ensuing pricing dynamics.

Recall that the latest “Panther Lake” Core Ultra Series 3 chip – Intel’s first commercial client product based on Foundry’s proprietary 18A node – began volume production exiting 2025 and is on track to start shipping in consumer laptops later this month. CCG is also eyeing the launch of additional SKUs, including Panther Lake-powered edge systems, through the first half of the year to “solidify Intel’s strong position in the notebook segment across both consumer and enterprise” end-markets.

This roadmap is further complemented by the continued ramp of Intel’s current “Arrow Lake” Core Ultra Series 2 client processor, which is optimized for high-end desktop applications. A follow-up refresh is slated to start shipping later this year, with the product expected to deliver greater memory support that will unlock up to 12.5% better memory speed than its standard offering. CCG is also expected to launch its next-generation “Nova Lake” Core Ultra 400 Series based on all-new architecture and software upgrades optimized for high-end desktop applications, which is slated to start volume production later this year and ramp into 2027.

Taken together, CCG’s forward roadmap will underpin Intel’s “strongest PC portfolio in years”. Yet the planned go-to-market timeline now looks increasingly precarious, as near-term memory roadblocks represent an imminent gating factor for PC demand and risk undermining CCG’s unit economics. This suggests CCG could be at risk of shipping its most advanced roadmap into a client compute downturn, introducing uncertainties to the durability of FCF underpinning Intel’s broader turnaround strategy.

2. Heightened Foundry ROI Uncertainties

As mentioned earlier, Intel Foundry remains a key pillar to the company’s turnaround plan. It’s also a key focus area for investors, corroborated by the stock’s momentum since Nvidia’s strategic announcement and next-generation chip collaboration with Intel; the U.S. government’s policy support for domestic chip manufacturing; and speculation for potential 18A uptake by Microsoft for its next-generation “Maia 2” AI processor – all of which are relevant and critical to Foundry’s long-term ROI prospects.

Specifically, the 18A node remains a focal point of Foundry’s strategy, which management has repeatedly emphasized will primarily facilitate internal volumes from Intel Products’ roadmap. This is also consistent with management’s recent remarks that 18A’s leading customer has “long been Intel itself”, with the node expected to represent the “foundation for at least [the] next three generations of client and server products”. This includes the 18A-based Panther Lake and Nova Lake client processors and the upcoming Clearwater Forest Xeon 6+ server CPU. Coupled with ancillary demand from the U.S. government under the $3 billion Secure Enclave contract and contributions from “other committed customers” that could potentially include Microsoft under an initial agreement signed during the Gelsinger era, management expects 18A to “drive wafer volumes well into the next decade and generate a healthy return on investment”.

Yet the emerging memory supply crisis could risk disrupting the intended 18A unit economics and breakeven timeline – especially given heavy reliance on end-market demand for CCG’s impending roadmap to scale the technology. Specifically, under Intel’s previous leadership led by Gelsinger, Intel Foundry was targeting “breakeven operating margins midway between now [2024] and the end of 2030”, aligning with current industry expectations for the unit’s earnings inflection to happen around 2027. Yet Foundry remains in heavy losses, with an operating margin of -134% in Q3 reflecting its earnings inflection’s dependence on the successful ramp of upcoming 18A-based products. This is further corroborated by current management’s expectation for long-term margin tailwinds as 18A scales the upcoming go-to-market of CCG’s next-generation portfolio:

We should see gross margins improve on the foundry side for sure. Partly, that is the scale dynamic that we will see benefit from, but also as we move towards more leading-edge mix, 18A for sure, but also even Intel 4, 3, those products have better pricing and a better cost structure. And so those margins should be accretive. And really, the dynamic about how much it improves will largely be a function of how the mix plays out through the year.

Source: Intel Q3 2025 Earnings Call Transcript

This setup accordingly raises the stakes around Intel’s prioritized CCG roadmap this year. Not only are Panther Lake and Nova Lake contributing to Intel’s strongest PC portfolio in years, but they also play a critical role in reinforcing Foundry’s ROI visibility. However, adverse memory dynamics now risk renewed uncertainty around Foundry’s earnings prospects – and at an inopportune time, given increased investors’ urgency for Intel to deliver. As a result, the emerging memory constraint over PC end-market demand could represent an incremental downside risk to existing execution hurdles that Intel’s recovery roadmap already faces.

Taken together, the urgency for Intel to secure external uptake on Foundry volume has effectively increased. As discussed in earlier sections, the upcoming collaboration with Nvidia on next-generation chips, including custom x86 CPUs, as well as speculation around Microsoft’s potential uptake of 18A for its Maia 2 processor, have been key drivers of the Intel stock’s recent valuation re-rate. Given the impending risks of disruption to internal volumes, expectations for Intel to deliver soon on these external opportunities are now even higher.

Fundamental and Price Considerations

I’m maintaining my base case price target of $31 per share for Intel, which implies a downside of 36% from the stock’s current levels (January 20 close).

This reflects my foregoing analysis that the impending memory headwinds on the PC end-market demand could add to Intel’s execution hurdles ahead, heightening risks of correction to its valuation premium to adjust for tempered growth expectations and Foundry ROI realization ahead.

In the downside scenario, CCG could revert to mid-single digit percentage declines in the current year, lining up with current industry estimates for PC unit TAM shipments in 2026. This could push out the unit’s growth roadmap and delay Foundry’s earnings inflection, resulting in lower FCF underpinning the stock’s valuation outlook.

Under the downside scenario forecast, the stock could face a correction towards $19 per share. The downside scenario price target is computed under the discounted cash flow method and considers projections taken from the related fundamental forecast above.

It considers an estimated terminal value of $116.2 billion, which is computed by the application of a 1.5% perpetual growth rate on the downside scenario terminal cash flow proxied by 2030E EBITDA. The 1.5% perpetual growth rate assumption aligns with the lower range of long-term economic expansion across Intel’s core operating markets and reflects the tempered pace of sustained FCF accretion as its current business plan ramps towards maturity.

Admittedly, management’s expectation for the 18A node to underpin Foundry volumes through the next decade implies an extended trajectory of ROI accretion. However, the downside scenario valuation assumption reflects a tempered outlook, consistent with expectations for a delayed ROI realization trajectory stemming from potentially protracted memory headwinds ahead. Total downside scenario cash flow projections, including the terminal value assumption, are discounted at a WACC of 8.9%, in line with Intel’s capital structure and risk profile.

Upside Catalysts to Consider

Despite the emerging risks of memory disruption to Intel’s growth and earnings profile, the related downside could be offset by execution on previously discussed deliveries – particularly on external Foundry uptake. This would be consistent with management’s commitment to “going big time into 14A” earlier this month, improving from earlier commentary from Tan that related investments remain dependent on end-market demand. The update also suggests tangible follow-up from Q3 earnings commentary that references improving customer engagements for 14A and reinforces prospects that Intel has secured external interest for its next-generation node:

Since the last balance quarter, I think clearly, our engagement with the customer for 14A has increased, and we are very heavily engaging with the customer in terms of defining the technology, the process, the yield, and the IP requirement to serve them. And they clearly see the tremendous demand that they need to have Intel to be strong on the 14A. And so we are delighted and more confident.

Source: Intel Q3 2025 Earnings Call Transcript

However, this could also introduce near-term trade-offs to Intel’s earnings profile. Specifically, the company’s current capital investment trajectory – which includes management’s guidance for $18 billion gross capex in 2025 – likely doesn’t account for 14A requirements yet. This is in line with management’s recent commentary and Tan’s Foundry plan that Intel will remain “very disciplined around capex”, with related spending to stay demand-dependent:

I think as you look at CapEx, it puts us in a position of flexibility on CapEx, but we want to be very disciplined around CapEx. So we will absolutely be looking at demand. Lip-Bu's been very direct with us on this. He wants to see the whites of the eyes of the customer so that we can believe in that demand. And if that demand exists, of course, we will amp up the CapEx as necessary.

Source: Intel Q3 2025 Earnings Call Transcript

As a result, if Intel’s confirmed external commitment to Foundry’s next-generation node, then it could suggest additional capital requirements to fund related R&D and capacity ramp-up ahead. Coupled with looming memory headwinds facing 18A’s ROI prospects, this could suggest risks of further delays to Foundry breakeven, maintaining the earnings overhang on the stock, and threatening durability to its current premium.

Another counterargument to my downside thesis for Intel also includes ongoing tariff uncertainties that could favour Foundry’s U.S.-based capacity advantage. Specifically, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick has recently put out a reminder that the risk of up to 100% semiconductor tariffs remains on the table for chipmakers that’ve yet to invest or build in the U.S. However, with Intel yet to translate existing policy support into tangible operating results, the urgency to deliver this year remains elevated – especially following the stock’s pre-execution appreciation observed through 2H25.

Final Thoughts

It’s evident that Intel’s turnaround prospects remain precarious. While observations of external validation for Foundry last year have been a booster to investor confidence, the credibility of Intel’s execution capability remains on the line. Recently stiffening memory headwinds and their adverse implications on PC demand dynamics ahead also risk adding another layer of execution challenge for Intel.

Specifically, rising memory costs and the ensuing threat to PC affordability for key enterprise and consumer end-markets could directly pressure CCG’s roadmap, and by extension, the funding base required to sustain Foundry’s growth investments. CCG’s success also remains closely tied to Foundry’s scalability, given 18A’s significant dependency on returns driven by internal volumes from Intel Products, highlighting the potential impact of looming memory headwinds.

Taken together, the emerging memory challenge could risk repeating a familiar Intel pattern – namely, the introduction of a strong technology roadmap that risks shipping into an unfavourable demand backdrop. This could potentially complicate Intel’s fragile turnaround, which continues to lack tangible progress.

As Intel heads into its Q4 earnings update later this week (January 22), investors’ focus will likely shift towards the company’s ability to execute on the previous open items that’ve buoyed the stock’s recent appreciation. There’ll likely be increased demand for CCG resilience as well to offset steepening execution challenges ahead and maintain durability to the stock’s current premium.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: YMAG: A High-Yield Fund-Of-Funds Play On Mag 7 Stocks
data.description:
The YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund of Option Income ETF (YMAG) is a potentially appealing (unleveraged) fund-of-funds type investment option for investors that want to generate high weekly income from an ETF with underlying Magnificent 7 exposure. The YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund of Option Income ETF has an attractive distribution yield of more than 32% (based off of the last declared and annualized distribution of $0.0857/share) and also achieved strong total returns since its inception in January 2024. If you believe that Magnificent 7 companies are going to continue to out-perform in the future, due to a high AI-driven CapEx spending and the growing adoption of AI products and services across the enterprise landscape, YMAG may be an interesting investment option for you.

Fund profile and investment strategy

The YMAG is a relatively new invention and has an operating history just shy of two years. The inception of the YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund of Option Income ETF gives investors the opportunity to monetize a long position in Magnificent 7 companies which include Nvidia (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), and Tesla (TSLA) all of which have considerable AI exposure in their core businesses.

To name just one example, Nvidia is seeing explosive growth in its revenues, cash flows and earnings due to accelerating demand for its GPUs that are used in Data Centers around the world to power large language models and AI applications. In my recent work on Nvidia I argued that the GPU maker is set to see a major upswing in its growth in the coming years due to a new, massive growth opportunity: Sovereign AI.

Additionally, Magnificent 7 stocks such as Nvidia, Alphabet and Amazon have all done really well lately and achieved new all-time highs, due to growing AI-related product portfolios and surging investor interest. Magnificent 7 stocks are, given the secular nature of AI spending, uniquely suited for option writing strategies. With CapEx spending especially surging, AI stocks should be able to maintain their growth momentum in 2026 as well.

YMAG's investment strategy leans into this overall AI theme and revolves around owning seven specific covered call YieldMax ETFs that are equally weighted in the ETF's portfolio and are rebalanced monthly. The YMAG does not directly own shares in the Magnificent 7 companies, however, but the covered call ETFs in YMAG's portfolio create synthetic long positions in the Magnificent 7 firms through derivatives.

The main idea behind owning a fund-of-funds option like YMAG is that demand for artificial intelligence is going to increase across the industry landscape which is going to lead to growing revenues and cash flows for Magnificent 7 companies specifically. This strong growth trajectory makes them uniquely suitable for call option strategies that generate recurring income for investors. The YMAG utilizes an option overlay strategy with each individual ETF included in the portfolio writing its own call options. YMAG charges investors an 1.12% gross expense ratio to manage their capital which is about in-line with the expense ratios of other Yieldmax ETFs.

YMAG's portfolio currently has a total investment value of $385M which makes it one of the smaller ETFs that YieldMax has started in recent years. A rival ETF to the YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund of Option Income ETF is the Roundhill Magnificent Seven Income Strategy ETF (MAGY) which currently manages about $222M and is therefore significantly smaller than YMAG.

The MAGY uses a similar investment strategy than YMAG, but the main difference is that the former only owns shares of the Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS) in its portfolio and writes call options only on shares of MAGS as a result. You can read a more detailed presentation of MAGY in my work: Monetize Mag 7 Stocks With This Covered Call ETF.

The main difference between these two ETFs is that YMAG relies on synthetic option-based income strategies through its underlying YieldMax ETFs, while MAGY utilizes traditional covered calls. The MAGY writes out-of-the-money call options on the underlying MAGS which makes the option writing strategy more simple and forward than the one the YMAG relies on.

The YMAG is a fund-of-fund ETF whose underlying YieldMax ETF use synthetic long exposure and call spreads which means the ETF's participation in upside gains is more limited (up to the strike price). Since MAGY participates directly in the underlying ETF's gains, it may be a preferred investment during strong market rallies, led by tech. The YMAG, on the other hand, is optimized for high option income which is why it is chiefly appealing to investors more focused on aggressive weekly income generation today.

The last declared weekly distribution is $0.0857/share, which will be paid to investors on 01/22/2026. Therefore, YMAG currently has a distribution yield of 32% while the 30-day SEC yield stands at 64%. However, YMAG's effective distribution yield is going to change drastically over time as the ETF announces changing dividend amounts each week: in the last year, YMAG has paid a dividend as low as $0.05/share (January 2026) and as high as $0.31/share (January 2025).

Performance profile

The YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund of Option Income ETF has done well so far and achieved a 60.97% cumulative net asset value return since its inception on January 29, 2024. This calculation assumes that investors reinvested their distributions into new shares of YMAG. While tech stocks have done well lately, a correction in the technology market could lead to serious downside potential for YMAG investors so investors should not extrapolate the ETF's year-to-life investment results into the future.

YMAG's valuation

The YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund of Option Income ETF has seen high double-digit net asset value growth since the start of 2024 and the ETF's total return profile is also impressive: since inception, the YMAG has delivered a total return of 37.5% compared to a total return of 25.1% for MAGY.

Since high-yielding covered call ETFs often see net asset value declines, if their distributions exceed what they earn from their option writing strategies, a key risk for investors is a phenomenon called "NAV erosion." Since inception, the YMAG has lost approximately 30.3% of its net asset value, so investors contemplating a purchase of this high-yielding ETF need to be aware of the risk of capital erosion over a longer period of time.

Risks with YMAG

The YMAG directly owns a number of covered call ETFs. These ETFs write call options in order to capture premium income which means that the ETF has limited upside potential long term and is at serious risk of under-performing Magnificent 7 companies during strong market rallies. This is because option overlay strategies require the option seller to deliver shares at a lower price then market price if the option buyer exercises its call option... which tend to happen in the event that stock prices increase sharply in the short term.

Therefore, investors that have a favorable outlook on large-cap U.S. tech companies may be better off actually owning shares of the Magnificent 7 companies directly. Further, high-yielding ETFs like YMAG are subject to NAV erosion meaning if they pay out more in dividends than they earn from their call writing strategies, investors face the risk of a deteriorating capital base.

Final thoughts

The YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund of Option Income ETF is, despite its shortcomings and risks, a potentially attractive investment option for investors that want to combine a positive, general outlook on Magnificent 7 companies with an option overlay strategy to monetize this long position and generate high, weekly income.

The YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund of Option Income ETF has done quite well since its inception on a total returns basis, despite a sliding net asset value... which is the result of the ETF's aggressive call writing approach. I believe the underlying theme of artificial intelligence will make the ETF a formidable force in the market going forward, especially as CapEx and revenue trends make them uniquely suitable for call writing strategies.

data.title: Richtech Robotics Is A Dilution Trap (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

The first time I broke down Richtech Robotics Inc. (RR) was back in October, as I was looking for a robotic stock to invest in myself. After reviewing the company's numbers, I gave it a Hold rating and noted that a $1.1 billion market cap, combined with low single-digit revenue, did not make much sense. I remind you, the stock of the company has traded at about 280x EV/sales, 10 to 20 times higher than most of the robotics competitors. Since then, the valuation has cooled down, and now the market cap is below $800 million, with 166 EV/sales on a trailing twelve-month basis.

Not that it’s cheap, but it’s definitely better, and together with the fact that the company just released its 10-K for 2025, I think it’s the perfect moment to go through that filing. And, oh boy, did I find it interesting.

I don’t remember the last time I read the entire 10-K, and I was reading it faster and faster as my eyes widened from the disbelief. Richtech Robotics managed to surprise me once again, but not to impose my personal opinion on you, I’ll walk you through the report so you can see the numbers for yourself. Before getting to the section where I explain my standpoint on the findings, you will make up your own mind. Let’s call it an interactive article, because I am genuinely curious whether my reaction is proportionate.

10-K 2025

Going through Richtech Robotics' fiscal year 2025, we see that the company ended the year with net revenue of $5.05 million. It was up by about 19% from the $4.24 million one year ago. If you break that down to see where the money is coming from, product sales brought in $2.31 million, while leasing and services added $1.43 million, and their Robots-as-a-Service (RaaS) model contributed just shy of $700,000.

Jumping from here to the income statement, we see that after accounting for the cost of revenue at $1.76 million, the company was left with a gross profit of roughly $3.29 million, which was actually a better result percentage-wise than the last year. According to my calculation, it means that the gross margin of the company expanded by about 100 basis points. Research and Development spending increased but only slightly to $2.43 million from $2.02 million, while Sales and Marketing expenses actually decreased to $1.26 million from $1.32 million. General and Administrative costs surged to $17.54 million, a massive increase from the $6.46 million spent in 2024. When you subtract total operating expenses from the gross profit, you get a loss from operations of $17.94 million and a net loss for the year of $15.75 million.

Despite the losses, the company’s bank account looks drastically different than it did a year ago. They ended 2025 with $193.6 million in cash, while a year ago its cash position had been at about $14.6 million.

We already know that this cash didn’t come from robot sales from the income statement, so we have to look for another table to see where it came from.

It came from financing activities, which poured in a net $236.1 million, mostly through selling Class B shares via "At-The-Market" offerings. Speaking of shares, the dilution is visible in the count, with about 40 million Class A shares and over 175 million Class B shares outstanding as of January 2026.

Applicable percentage ownership is based on an aggregate of 215,095,973 shares of our common stock, consisting of (i) 39,934,846 shares of our Class A common stock and (ii) 175,161,127 shares of our Class B common stock outstanding as of January 20, 2026. We have determined beneficial ownership in accordance with the rules of the SEC. - 10-K 2025

Here is a quote from my previous article for the comparison:

As of August 2025, there were about 39.9 million Class A shares and 113 million Class B shares, representing 153 million total votes.

On the human capital, the company is running with a team of 55 full-time employees in the United States, mostly based in Nevada. The breakdown of that team shows the focus is heavily weighted toward engineering, with 27 people in R&D, compared to 12 in Sales and Marketing, 8 in Administration, and just a handful in Operations and Tech Support. They also supplement this with 20 overseas R&D staff, primarily out of China.

This was pretty much the main part of the 10-K that was worth noticing. And now, let me tell you how I view those results.

Company Overview

The first question I had when I saw the revenue mix was how it could be that leasing and rental were down while RaaS is up? Isn’t it the same as leasing and rental? The answer is nuanced, and while there is nothing particularly bad, I really don’t like it when the company starts “playing with the numbers” on their books, even if legally they don’t cross any borders.

So what happened there is that they decided to label the revenue streams differently this year versus last year. According to the filing, "Leasing/Service/Rental" and "RaaS" are treated as two different sources. The "Leasing" category will be a category for all the short-term contracts, including maintenance and installation fees, whereas under the "RaaS," you will find their long-term operating agreements. And it would not be a big deal if it did not change the way this company recognizes the revenue, because from here, we’ve got the expanded margins.

Gross profit increased by $569 thousand, or approximately 20.9%, from $2,720 thousand in 2024 to $3,289 thousand in 2025. The resulting expansion of our gross margin is a direct reflection of the full-year impact of the strategic shift to a RaaS model. By capitalizing the cost of leased assets rather than recognizing them as immediate cost of goods sold, our gross margin profile has significantly improved, leading to a higher gross profit despite the ongoing business model transition.

Translating this to simple language, they used an accounting trick to lift their gross margins. When they sell a robot, they have to subtract the full cost of building it immediately, which hurts their profit for that period. But under the new "Robots-as-a-Service" rental model, they get to include manufacturing cost on the balance sheet as an asset and only subtract a small part of it each month over time as depreciation and amortization. It makes their gross profit look significantly higher today, even though in practice, they spend the same amount of money, but they are not supposed to recognize it straight away. While this is not forbidden, a move like this can easily go unnoticed by the market, confusing real growth with shifts in accounting strategy.

In my previous article, my main critique point of this company was the fact that they spend too little on R&D and too much on other expenses that do not contribute to the real innovation and growth of this company in the future:

Spending nearly $14 million in G&A with only $3.6 million in revenue is not sustainable, especially when R&D remains barely above $1 million. If Richtech wants to compete in robotics long-term, it needs to stop throwing money on short-term marketing and start heavily working on innovation, because otherwise, this will be the company that will run out of fuel in a few years and go bankrupt. Without new technologies, no one would even want to acquire them.

I thought it was already bad then, but I couldn’t imagine it would become so much worse. The company slightly increased the R&D spending year-over-year, meaning almost no spending in the last quarter of 2025, because the R&D in September for the year 2025 had already been at $2.4 million. How this company creates something without investing in it is beyond me. Marketing decreased, but only a little, which is actually good, taking into account how huge the expense already was before.

But what happened with the General and Administrative expenses? This is the expense that I, as an investor, don’t want to see ballooning at all. This is the type of expense that doesn’t help the company with its operating activities and doesn’t make the product better. In this category, you usually have: Rent, Utilities, Insurance, Executives' wages and benefits, Office fixtures and equipment (not related to manufacturing), Legal counsel and accounting staff salaries, Office supplies.

The company states that the increase is primarily due to legal, audit, and consulting costs as a result of being a public company and the requirements for all the SEC filings, etc. But if you actually look at the statement, you would see that they are not required to provide checks to where the majority of the money actually went. Not to accuse anyone of anything, I want to underline that throughout the whole year of 2024, the company has already been public and had the same requirements for legal paperwork.

They also say they had additional spending on issuance, which could happen, although the ballooning of the expense is too big to justify in my view. So the most probable cause, in my opinion, and it is also stated in the 10-K, is the purchase of the new corporate headquarters and the costs for moving.

Now, I have a serious concern that this has nothing to do with the growth of the company and the real need for that improvement. To address this concern, I found the filing of the company from a year ago, for fiscal 2024. It turns out they not only have fewer employees today than they did back then, but I was once again disappointed to see that their R&D department shrank by 25% in a year. How does that make sense for the innovative company that positions itself to make any kind of breakthrough? I also read that the management says they have difficulties in hiring talent, especially in R&D, but I have an idea why they encounter such a problem - it looks like they have a huge employee turnover ratio, and based on the feedback online on the trusted websites, I conclude that this is not the best place to work in.

If I were a shareholder, I would be seriously disappointed by the enormous dilution of my value for covering something as insignificant as a new headquarters.

I also ask myself what this enormous cash position is for. New furniture is great, but it looks like this company is planning on a larger expense than that. On one hand, in the best interest of shareholders, I would hope that the plan is to make a strategic acquisition, since the company shrinks its R&D department and doesn’t seem to be innovating by itself. On the other hand, their return from investing is negative, so where are any guarantees that the money will be spent correctly and that the execution of this acquisition will be solid?

Valuation

About the valuation, the stock is majorly overvalued, and even though the revenue is growing, a 20% growth rate is nowhere near justifying this premium. Looking at the expenses, in my view this company is apparently not prioritizing shareholder value or innovation. I am not convinced about all the products and developments this company has. It states that the product is deployed to many customers throughout the U.S., yet I was unable to find a single piece of feedback on how it works and how much value it brings to the businesses, which I find odd.

Conclusion

Taking into account everything, I found this RR breakdown really difficult, as the numbers show a story that is hard to tell without reading too much into it. I am not an insider, and it’s possible I interpret the numbers in the wrong way, but being honest, this doesn’t look good to me as an investor. Repeating the beginning of this piece, I don’t remember when I was reading the report with such enthusiasm, not believing what I saw. The company managed to beat the expectations, and it looks like the stock is climbing higher on the news (it's up by almost 2% on the premarket as I am writing this), but I see something entirely different. I know betting against the hyped stocks in hot industries is burning your money, but Richtech Robotics, as it is today, doesn’t deserve any premium, let alone 5000% on the sector median.

data.title: Thornburg Investment Income Builder Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
This note will highlight the results of the Thornburg Investment Income Builder Fund for Q4 2025. It is published amidst the ongoing developments associated with the Russian war with Ukraine, ongoing inflation concerns in the U.S., and significant government policy changes in the U.S. and elsewhere that are altering global trade and financial flows. Most equity indices worldwide delivered positive returns in the December quarter and for calendar 2025. Returns from bonds in the U.S. were positive.

Thornburg Investment Income Builder Fund (TIBIX) paid ordinary dividends of $0.42 per I share during Q4’2025, flat versus Q4’24. For calendar 2025 your fund paid ordinary dividends of $1.27 per I share, +2.6% vs the prior year. The dividends per share were lower for A and C shares, to account for varying class specific expenses. Your fund also paid a long-term capital gains dividend of 66.2¢/share in November 2025. Including the capital gains dividends, trailing 4 quarter dividends of $1.93 per I share were +29.5% vs the combined dividend of $1.49 per share for the 4 quarters ending December 31, 2024.

Approximately 90% of the dividends paid by your fund in 2024 were characterized as “Qualifying Dividends” for federal income tax purposes, leading to a lower tax rate on these payments. When 2025 calculations are finalized, we expect a slightly smaller percentage of your fund’s 2025 distributions to also be qualifying dividends. More than 75% of your Investment Income Builder’s equity holdings increased dividends in local currencies in 2025. A weaker $US was a tailwind to your fund’s dividend growth in calendar 2025, though reduced special dividend payments were a head- wind. Other factors may impact the distributable cash total.

Readers of this commentary who are long-time shareholders of Thornburg Investment Income Builder will recall that the interest-bearing debt portion of the fund’s portfolio has varied over time, ranging from less than 9% in 2015 to 45% on June 30, 2009. We tend to allocate more portfolio assets to interest-bearing debt when debt yields are more attractive. Approximately 16% of portfolio assets were invested in interest-bearing debt and near cash assets on December 31, 2025. The remaining 84% of portfolio assets were invested in equities as previously described.

Earnings and other valuation multiples for most segments of the U.S. equity market are above historic averages though sector multiples vary across the globe. Real wages after inflation for a large segment of U.S. households are not increasing. The absolute number of unemployed workers in the U.S. has been rising, making additional Federal Funds rate cuts probable despite inflation running above Federal Reserve targets. Recent progress to reach the Fed’s 2% inflation target has been difficult to achieve, complicated by uncertainty around tariffs and curtailments in the number of foreign-born workers in the U.S. labor force. A large supply of new government debt being issued plus ongoing corporate borrowing present challenges to further reductions in longer maturity bond yields unless the U.S. economy weakens considerably in the coming quarters.

Incoming economic data from around the world indicate an uncertain global economy in the face of evolving U.S. trade policies, so far resisting falling into a recession. Global trade flows are being altered by new U.S. trade policies. Winners and losers among multinational producers of tradeable goods and developers of artificial intelligence capabilities will become obvious in time, but the current outlook for many global businesses is uncertain. Prices of traded financial assets will be volatile with day-to-day news changing perceptions of near-term economic performance, interest rate policies, and political decisions. We are optimistic about the future return potential of Thornburg Investment Income Builder’s assets. Why?

Virtually all the businesses in your portfolio retain their market positions providing important products and services that generate cash flows to pay attractive dividends, today and in the future. We believe they are valued very attractively in relation to their own histories and relative to other assets.

The weighted harmonic average 2025 consensus expected price/earnings ratio for Thornburg Investment Income Builder’s equity portfolio, tabulated using FactSet data, was approximately 14.2x as of December 31, 2025, well below the 21.6x price/earnings ratio of the MSCI All Country World Index using 2025 expected EPS. We believe your Investment Income Builder portfolio incorporates significant intrinsic value.
